title: Deutsches Technikmuseum: Quick Summary
items: **The Deutsches Technikmuseum is Berlin's biggest museum complex**, opened in 1983 on the old Anhalter freight yard in Kreuzberg. It spans 19 permanent exhibitions across a glass New Building, two real 1874 railway roundhouses, the Ladestraße depot and a museum park, with a Douglas C-47 Rosinenbomber parked on the roof since May 1999.

**Tickets: €15 online, €17 at the door, and everyone under 18 is free** (book the free 0-euro ticket per child). Reduced tickets are €9 online. The Museum Pass Berlin is accepted, and one day ticket covers both the main museum and the hands-on Science Center Spectrum.

**Closed Mondays.** Open Tuesday to Friday 9:00–17:30 and weekends 10:00–18:00, with last admission at 17:00. On the first Friday of every month entry is free for everyone from 13:00 with an online booking.

**There are two entrances**: Trebbiner Straße 9 for the main building and Möckernstraße 26 for the Ladestraße and Spectrum. Take the U1, U3 or U7 to Möckernbrücke, the U1, U2 or U3 to Gleisdreieck, or the S-Bahn to Anhalter Bahnhof.

**Do not try to see everything.** The average visit is about 2.5 hours in a place that can fill a day, so pick three or four halls: Aviation, the rail roundhouses, Shipping, and Spectrum if you have kids. The Train Builder in the article couples them into a plan that fits your time.
